section.title: Woe to Ariel
section.sectionRationale: Continuing the woes, chapters 28 to 33, this oracle turns to Jerusalem herself, called Ariel, besieged by the LORD and yet suddenly delivered from her attackers. It exposes worship that is mere lip service, a sealed scroll no one can read, and a people who hide their plans from God as if He, the potter, could be deceived by His own clay.
section.thesis: The LORD besieges Ariel, His own city, exposing worship that honors Him with lips while hearts stay far away, yet He will suddenly deliver her from her attackers, unseal the scroll, open deaf ears and blind eyes, and bring the humble to rejoice in the Holy One of Israel.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the siege of Ariel and her sudden deliverance from the nations; the sealed scroll and worship that is mere lip service; woe to those who hide their plans from the LORD, the potter mistaken for clay; and the promised day when the deaf hear, the blind see, and the humble rejoice.

units.label: The siege of Ariel and her sudden deliverance
units.note: besieged, then rescued like waking from a dream

verses.text: Woe to you, O {{ariel|Ariel}}, the city of Ariel where David camped! Year upon year let your festivals recur.
And I will constrain Ariel, and there will be mourning and lamentation; she will be like an altar hearth before Me.
I will camp in a circle around you; I will besiege you with towers and set up siege works against you.
You will be brought low, you will speak from the ground, and out of the dust your words will be muffled. Your voice will be like a spirit from the ground; your speech will whisper out of the dust.
But your many foes will be like fine dust, the multitude of the ruthless like blowing chaff. Then suddenly, in an instant,
you will be visited by the LORD of Hosts with thunder and earthquake and loud noise, with windstorm and tempest and consuming flame of fire.
All the many nations going out to battle against Ariel, even all who war against her, laying siege and attacking her, will be like a dream, like a vision in the night,

as when a hungry man dreams he is eating, then awakens still hungry; as when a thirsty man dreams he is drinking, then awakens faint and parched. So will it be for all the many nations who go to battle against Mount Zion.

units.label: The sealed scroll, worship as lip service

verses.text: Stop and be astonished; blind yourselves and be sightless; be drunk, but not with wine; stagger, but not from strong drink.
For the LORD has poured out on you a spirit of deep sleep. He has shut your eyes, O prophets; He has covered your heads, O seers.
And the entire vision will be to you like the words {{chatam|sealed}} in a scroll. If it is handed to someone to read, he will say, “I cannot, because it is sealed.”
Or if the scroll is handed to one unable to read, he will say, “I cannot read.”
Therefore the Lord said: “These people draw near to Me with their mouths and honor Me with their lips, but their hearts are far from Me. Their worship of Me is but rules taught by men.
Therefore I will again confound these people with wonder upon wonder. The wisdom of the wise will vanish, and the intelligence of the intelligent will be hidden.”

units.label: Woe to those who hide from the LORD

verses.text: Woe to those who dig deep to hide their plans from the LORD. In darkness they do their works and say, “Who sees us, and who will know?”

You have turned things upside down, as if the potter were regarded as clay. Shall what is formed say to him who formed it, “He did not make me”? Can the pottery say of the potter, “He has no understanding”?
In a very short time, will not Lebanon become an orchard, and the orchard seem like a forest?

units.label: The deaf hear, the blind see, the humble rejoice

verses.text: On that day the deaf will hear the words of the scroll, and out of the deep darkness the eyes of the blind will see.
The humble will increase their joy in the LORD, and the poor among men will rejoice in the Holy One of Israel.
For the ruthless will vanish, the mockers will disappear, and all who look for evil will be cut down,
those who indict a man with a word, who ensnare the mediator at the gate, and who with false charges deprive the innocent of justice.
Therefore the LORD who redeemed Abraham says of the house of Jacob: “No longer will Jacob be ashamed and no more will his face grow pale.
For when he sees his children around him, the work of My hands, they will honor My name, they will sanctify the Holy One of Jacob, and they will stand in awe of the God of Israel.
Then the wayward in spirit will come to understanding, and those who grumble will accept instruction.”

ariel.gloss: A name for Jerusalem meaning altar hearth, or possibly lion of God, playing on the fire that consumes sacrifices on the altar. Naming the besieged city Ariel casts the coming siege itself as a strange kind of offering, the city itself becoming like an altar hearth before the LORD.

chatam.gloss: To seal, to close up so the contents cannot be read or accessed until properly opened. The vision sealed like a scroll no one can read pictures spiritual blindness so complete that God's own revelation becomes unintelligible, an image Revelation later reverses when only the Lamb is found worthy to open the sealed scroll, Revelation 5.

basic.context: Jerusalem, called Ariel, faces a siege so severe that she is compared to a voice whispering from the dust, yet the LORD suddenly and dramatically delivers her, so that her many attackers vanish like a dream upon waking. At the same time, the LORD exposes worship that is mere lip service, a sealed scroll that no one can read, and rulers who scheme in secret as if the potter could not see His own clay.
basic.hermeneutics: The siege and sudden deliverance of Ariel most plausibly describes the historical rescue of Jerusalem from Sennacherib's Assyrian siege in 701 BC, when the city was miraculously spared, 2 Kings 19:35-36. The sealed scroll and hollow worship expose a real, ongoing spiritual condition, hearts far from God despite outward religion, which Jesus later quotes directly of the religious leaders of His own day, Matthew 15:8-9.
basic.originalLanguages: Jerusalem is called Ariel, likely altar hearth, casting the coming siege as a strange offering upon the city itself. The prophetic vision becomes chatham, sealed, entirely inaccessible to a people who can neither read nor comprehend it, the very image Revelation later reverses when the Lamb alone proves worthy to break the seals and open the scroll, Revelation 5:1-9.
basic.typology: The sealed scroll that no one can open or read anticipates the scroll in Revelation 5, sealed until the Lamb, Christ, alone is found worthy to open it. The promised day when the deaf hear and the blind see anticipates and is directly fulfilled in Christ's own healing ministry, cited by Jesus Himself as proof of His messianic identity, Matthew 11:4-5.
basic.christ: Jesus quotes verse 13 directly against the Pharisees, these people honor Me with their lips, but their hearts are far from Me, Matthew 15:8-9, exposing the same hollow religion centuries later. His healing ministry, opening deaf ears and blind eyes, Matthew 11:4-5, Luke 4:18, directly fulfills verse 18's promise, and He alone, the Lamb once slain, is found worthy to open the sealed scroll that no human being could open, Revelation 5:1-9.
basic.god: God sees straight through outward religious performance to the state of the heart, and He is not fooled by those who plot in secret, since the potter always knows His own clay. Yet even His exposure of hollow worship and secret scheming serves a redemptive purpose: to bring about the day when the deaf hear, the blind see, and the humble rejoice in the Holy One of Israel.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 19:35-36 records the historical, sudden deliverance of Jerusalem from Sennacherib's siege. Matthew 15:8-9 quotes Isaiah 29:13 directly against religious leaders whose worship is mere lip service. Matthew 11:4-5 records Jesus citing the healing of the deaf and blind as proof of His messianic identity. Revelation 5:1-9 pictures Christ, the Lamb, alone worthy to open the sealed scroll.
basic.oneStory: This chapter moves from a besieged, humbled city suddenly and miraculously delivered, through an exposure of empty religious performance, to the promised day when the deaf hear, the blind see, and the humble rejoice in God. Christ fulfills every strand: He quotes this chapter against hollow worship, heals the deaf and blind as this chapter foretold, and alone is worthy to unseal the scroll no human hand could open.

context.literary: Structured around escalating woes and reversals: a siege that ends in sudden deliverance, a scroll sealed against understanding that will one day be read by the deaf, and secret schemers exposed by the potter who made them, each movement contrasting present spiritual blindness with a coming day of sight and joy.
context.historical: Most plausibly set during the years before and during Sennacherib's siege of Jerusalem in 701 BC, when despite dire circumstances the city was miraculously spared, 2 Kings 19:35-36, Isaiah 37:36-37, an event that gives concrete historical weight to this chapter's dramatic language of sudden deliverance from many nations.
context.canonical: Its themes of a sealed, unreadable scroll and hollow worship echo forward into the New Testament, quoted directly by Jesus against religious hypocrisy, Matthew 15:8-9, and its healing and unsealing motifs are picked up in both the Gospels' accounts of Jesus' ministry and Revelation's vision of Christ opening the sealed scroll.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn Jerusalem that outward religious performance without genuine heart devotion leaves people spiritually blind and unable to understand God's revelation, while promising that the LORD who besieges can also deliver suddenly and will one day open both understanding and true worship to the humble.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter describes a specific historical siege and a specific spiritual condition, hollow worship and sealed understanding; it does not prescribe a formula for future sieges. Its abiding principle is that God is never deceived by outward religion divorced from the heart, and that true understanding and worship are ultimately gifts He gives rather than achievements of human wisdom.

debates.question: Who causes the scroll to be sealed and understanding to fail in verses 9 to 12, God Himself or the people's own hardened refusal?

views.label: God actively seals understanding as a judicial act
views.case: Verse 10 states plainly that the LORD has poured out on you a spirit of deep sleep and shut your eyes, suggesting an active, judicial hardening in response to persistent unfaithfulness, similar to Isaiah 6:9-10.

views.label: The people's own hollow worship and refusal to listen brings this blindness on themselves
views.case: Verse 13's diagnosis, they honor Me with their lips but their hearts are far from Me, suggests the sealing described is the natural, deserved consequence of choosing empty ritual over genuine devotion, with God's judicial action confirming rather than initiating their self-chosen blindness.

debates.bearing: Scripture elsewhere holds both truths together without contradiction: God's judicial hardening of persistently unfaithful hearts, Isaiah 6:9-10, Romans 1:24-28, is not arbitrary but a response to prior human rejection of the truth, so both readings describe the same reality from different angles.

study.originalLanguages: Jerusalem is named Ariel, likely altar hearth, so that her coming siege itself becomes a strange kind of offering before the LORD. Her spiritual condition is described as the vision becoming chatham, sealed, entirely closed off from a people who can neither read nor comprehend God's word, an image later reversed dramatically in Revelation when only the Lamb proves worthy to open the sealed scroll.

typology.type: The scroll sealed so that no one can read or understand it
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Lamb once slain, alone found worthy to open the sealed scroll, Revelation 5:1-9.

typology.type: The promised day when the deaf hear and the blind see
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' own healing ministry, cited by Him as proof of His messianic identity, Matthew 11:4-5, Luke 4:18-21.

study.christ: Jesus directly quotes verse 13 against the religious leaders of His day, these people honor Me with their lips, but their hearts are far from Me, Matthew 15:8-9, showing that the hollow worship this chapter exposes persisted right up to His own ministry. He then fulfills verse 18's promise in His own healing works, opening deaf ears and blind eyes as proof that He is the promised Messiah, Matthew 11:4-5. And where this chapter's sealed scroll left even the learned unable to understand God's revelation, Revelation 5 reveals that only Christ, the Lamb who was slain, is found worthy to break the seals and open what no human being could open.
study.god: God is not deceived by outward religious performance; He sees straight through lip-service worship to hearts that remain far from Him, and He knows the secret plans of those who imagine their darkness hides them from His sight, since the potter always knows His own clay. Yet even in exposing this hollowness, His ultimate purpose is redemptive, to bring the deaf to hear, the blind to see, and the humble to rejoice in Him as the Holy One of Israel.

crossReferences.note: Records the sudden, miraculous deliverance of Jerusalem from Sennacherib's siege, the likely historical background for Ariel's rescue in this chapter.
Jesus quotes Isaiah 29:13 directly against religious leaders whose worship was outward performance without true devotion of heart.
Jesus cites the healing of the deaf and blind, fulfilling this chapter's promise, as proof of His messianic identity.
Only Christ, the Lamb who was slain, is found worthy to open the sealed scroll that no one else could open, fulfilling this chapter's image of a scroll sealed against all understanding.

study.oneStory: This chapter moves from a besieged city suddenly and dramatically rescued, through the exposure of hollow, lip-service worship and a sealed, unreadable revelation, to the promised day when the deaf hear, the blind see, and the humble rejoice in the Holy One of Israel. Every strand finds its answer in Christ, who exposes hollow religion, heals the deaf and blind, and alone is worthy to unseal what no one else could open.

quiz.q: What ultimately happens to the many nations besieging Ariel, according to verses 5 to 8?

options.text: They vanish suddenly, like a dream from which someone wakes still hungry and thirsty.
options.note: Verses 7 to 8 describe exactly this sudden, dreamlike vanishing of the attacking nations.

options.text: They successfully conquer and permanently occupy Jerusalem.
options.note: The opposite; the nations' attack comes to nothing, like a vanished dream.

options.text: They negotiate a peaceful surrender with favorable terms.
options.note: No negotiation is described; the deliverance is sudden and dramatic, not a negotiated peace.

options.text: They retreat slowly over the course of several decades.
options.note: The text emphasizes suddenness, in an instant, not a slow, decades-long retreat.

quiz.q: What does the LORD say is the real problem with the people's worship in verse 13?

options.text: They honor Him with their lips, but their hearts are far from Him.
options.note: This is the exact diagnosis given, and it is later quoted directly by Jesus in Matthew 15:8-9.

options.text: They have stopped performing religious rituals altogether.
options.note: The opposite; they continue outward ritual and lip service, which is precisely the problem exposed.

options.text: They worship too many different gods at once.
options.note: Not the specific charge in this verse; the issue is insincerity in worship of the LORD, not polytheism per se.

options.text: They have replaced the temple with private home worship.
options.note: Not addressed in this verse; the issue named is hearts far from God despite outward worship, not the location of worship.

quiz.q: What analogy does verse 16 use to describe those who hide their plans from the LORD?

options.text: They act as if the potter could be regarded as mere clay, unable to know his own creation.
options.note: Verse 16 uses precisely this potter and clay analogy to expose their folly.

options.text: They act like sheep following a shepherd faithfully.
options.note: The opposite; the image used is one of foolish rebellion, not faithful following.

options.text: They act like builders constructing a strong tower.
options.note: Not the image used in this verse; the specific analogy is potter and clay.

options.text: They act like farmers planting seeds in good soil.
options.note: A different agricultural image appears elsewhere in Isaiah, but this specific verse uses the potter and clay analogy.

quiz.q: What reversal is promised in verse 18 for the deaf and the blind?

options.text: The deaf will hear the words of the scroll, and the blind will see out of deep darkness.
options.note: Verse 18 states this reversal explicitly, later fulfilled in Christ's healing ministry.

options.text: The deaf and blind will remain permanently unchanged.
options.note: The opposite; the verse promises a dramatic, hopeful reversal for them.

options.text: Only the wealthy deaf and blind will be healed.
options.note: No such economic restriction is mentioned in the text.

options.text: The deaf and blind will be exiled from the community.
options.note: The opposite of the text's promise, which is inclusion and restoration, not exile.

quiz.q: How does Jesus directly connect Himself to this chapter's themes?

options.text: He quotes verse 13 against hollow worship and fulfills verse 18 by healing the deaf and blind.
options.note: Matthew 15:8-9 quotes verse 13 directly, and Matthew 11:4-5 cites the healing of the deaf and blind as messianic proof.

options.text: He never references this chapter in any of the Gospels.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus directly quotes this chapter and fulfills its healing promise.

options.text: He only references it once in a minor, disputed parable.
options.note: The connection is a direct, undisputed quotation and a clear fulfillment, not a minor or disputed reference.

options.text: He references it solely to condemn the Gentile nations.
options.note: Jesus applies this chapter's critique to Jewish religious leaders and fulfills its healing promise broadly, not as a condemnation aimed at Gentiles.

questions: Jerusalem's attackers vanished suddenly, like waking from a dream. What overwhelming threat in your own life needs you to trust that God can dissolve it just as suddenly and completely?
The people honored God with their lips while their hearts stayed far from Him. Where might your own worship or religious habits have become more performance than genuine devotion?
Those who hid their plans from the LORD forgot that the potter always knows His own clay. What are you currently trying to hide from God that He already fully sees?
The promised reversal is that the deaf will hear and the blind will see. Where do you need God to open your own ears or eyes to something you have been missing or refusing to see?
The humble are promised increasing joy in the LORD. What would it look like this week to actively choose humility in a specific relationship or situation, trusting that joy, not loss, follows it?

translationNotes.note: Ariel is a name for Jerusalem whose meaning is debated, most likely altar hearth, though lion of God is also proposed; the wordplay casts the city's coming siege as a strange kind of sacrificial ordeal.
The sealed scroll here describes a vision made unintelligible to its hearers, an image later dramatically reversed in Revelation 5, where Christ alone is found worthy to open a sealed scroll.
This verse is quoted by Jesus in Matthew 15:8-9 against the Pharisees, applying Isaiah's ancient critique of hollow worship directly to religious leaders of His own day.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 29:1-24. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; no em dashes were present in the source text for this chapter.
